sentences.text: So I-- I went with this-- with this friends to-- to Odessa.
And we are going back.
And now he wants to stay in Kiev, and I want to go to a quiet place where I can recover the-- the war, because I was pregnant at that time.
I found out that I'm pregnant, and I wanted to be in a place which is calm.
And I have a choice because they know me at the Ministry of Health, so they gave me an assignment in Pereiaslav-- Pereiaslav, P-E-Y-I-A-S-L-A-V, Pereiaslav.
It's near Kiev.
It's near the Dnieper.
It's not so near Kiev, as it's near the Dnieper.
And there's-- they don't have any industry.
They don't have a train.
In other words, I asked for such an assignment.
So we got it, and we are in Kiev.

It-- it-- we are a week, and during this week, the police was taking us several times to the police, because we looked dressed differently than the Russians, and they were concerned about the fifth column, about spies.
So we are taken to the police and-- to give the account, why and what and so on.
So that finally, we took a boat and went to Pereiaslav and started to work in Pereiaslav.

In Pereiaslav, I found myself that my abdomen is getting bigger than the pregnancy, because I figured more or less that I might be pregnant two months, two and a half months, and it looks like six months.
So I had to go to a committee, and I start to tell them that I'm pregnant.
And they thought that probably I would have to have an operation, a laparotomy.
It means they'd open the-- they would have to open the belly, because they didn't think that it was a pregnancy.
But it turned out to be a pregnancy.
I needed-- I needed to have their permission to interrupt the pregnancy, which was done on medical-- on medical grounds.
And of course, this was something that was for me the hardest part of the whole war.
To say it in simple words, I said afterwards nothing already after this-- nothing is going to be difficult for me.
The cousin in-- in-- in Kiev didn't return to me the money, because he said it's wartime.
He's not going to pay now the money.
So I am without money because I didn't-- I didn't take any money with me, just for the trip.
So the job is something that is going now from now on to sustain me in Pereiaslav.
Again, I'll make it short.
In-- in September, I leave Pereiaslav.
In the meantime-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yeah, I leave Pereiaslav.
And I leave it together with this-- the nurse that worked under me in Pereiaslav, who was the commissar's wife.
So we got a truck, 'cause the-- the Germans moved in-- in Pereiaslav.
I was called to do-- to do surgery work to ambulatory soldiers who are-- were wounded.
No.
No.
Russians soldiers.
Russian soldiers.
They-- they were shooting from the other side of Dnieper.
Well, then we saw we were able to-- to go.
We went to the direction of Tamarovka.
We see they are going to replace us now.
So I went to Tamarovka, and there-- Tamarovka, T-A-M-A-R-O-V-K-A-- there, the Health Department, they needed a doctor in the village.
The village was the name Butovo, B-- B-U-T-O-V-O, Butovo.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And I'm given the position of a village doctor in charge of a section of a-- a country.
And the first patient that comes to see me was a woman who came with a little child who had a lack of thyroid.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had their tongue out, and so very easy to diagnose the situation.
So I tell her that-- I gave her a prescription of thyroid extract.
And she asked me, what's-- what's the matter with that child?
So I told her that it's a lack of thyroid.
And she was learning to be a midwife.

So she went home, and she took her notebook, and she found the description of exactly what's going on with her child in connection with this lack of thyroid gland or the insufficiency of the thyroid gland.
Well, that made me, immediately already, in that village [INAUDIBLE] the doctor who knows.
The previous doctor was taken to the-- and I have a big house which belonged to the-- the priests, the local priests, and they made a clinic out of it.
So I'm starting-- and I have there a horse with a carriage, like, on two wheels to-- to travel to see patients.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The horse name is Rosa Luxembourg.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [INAUDIBLE] the driver is-- [INAUDIBLE] at the same time [RUSSIAN].
It means he was distributing quinine for people who had malaria.
You know, three weeks I worked there.
After three weeks, the front comes closer.
Everyone has to leave.
The peasants came.
The local peasants came, asked me to stay.
They say they'll take care of me.
I have in the passport that I am Jewish, [RUSSIAN], but that doesn't matter to them.
I don't want to stay because I don't [INAUDIBLE] think I'm going to wait for the Germans to-- to come, so I'm going.
And now, the trip from Butovo.
In the meantime, in that Butovo-- and I have here those postcards.
In the meantime, I found a cousin-- my husband's cousin, the second daughter of that woman that is there with my relatives, with my in-laws.
And here the-- that I-- I found there Tamarovka [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, anyway, so I got her address in Syzran, and I got to them after a long travel.
The whole travel, I'm not going to go into it.
I got to Syzran, which I joined that cousin-- my husband's cousin, [?
Zonia's ?] cousin.
And there, I got a better job.
She didn't want me to get a job because her husband-- she preferred if I stay home with her.
They lived-- they left from Moscow to Syzran, he in connection with his work.
From there, I went with them, again, with the permission of the authorities, which I have here the documentation, went to Ufa.
And from Ufa, in November, I set out to go to Uzbekistan.

The reason I wanted to go to Uzbekistan-- Uzbekistan is close to-- to Iran and possibility to get out of-- of the Soviet Union and besides, to get somewhere where it's warm, because I didn't have any warm clothing, and it was early winter.
It was already a long time winter.
[INAUDIBLE] I'm not going to go into that, because we don't have time for this.
Briefly, I arrived to Uzbekistan.
It means that I arrived to Tashkent.
Tashkent is the capital of Uzbekistan.
Tashkent, near the train, there were an awful lot of people from different places.
At that time, they were talking about the Soviet Union as Russia on wheels.
And you didn't have a right to stay in Tashkent, so you couldn't.

[INAUDIBLE] And they didn't have enough positions for Russians then to-- to replace doctors in-- some-- in a hospital or in a clinic, because so many have come from all of Ukraine from-- and from Moscow and from wherever you want.
I am near the train, looking to see whether I'll find someone from my area, maybe my husband would come, or whatever.
People did find themselves, sometimes.
Each one was going somewhere.
So in [NON-ENGLISH], which is the health-- the health section of the ministry, they decided that they are going to send 50 doctors from those refugees to villages, kolhoz collective arms.
You know about the kolhoz.
I signed up on this list.
They put a list in the wall.
Who wants to go to kolhoz?
I signed up the second one, that list that I want to go to kolhoz.
But I didn't have, as a matter of fact, any choice.
And I was interviewed.
What-- the man that interviewed me, [INAUDIBLE] said what kind of a kolhoz I want.
So I said, I want one that is near a station-- near a train station.
So said he to me, was Kaganovich a good-- a good man?
I said, I don't have any reason to think that he was not.
Kaganovich was one of Stalin's helpers.
So he said, I'll send you to the kolhoz in the name of Kaganovich, which is near the station [INAUDIBLE], because I'm ask-- I asked him for near the station.
So-- so he gave me this-- that assignment, and I met-- and we-- we could take with us a nurse.
So I met around the station somewhere, a young woman who was from the area that I come from, in Volhynia [NON-ENGLISH].
And I suggested to her that maybe she'll go with me so then she will have work, and I will not be going-- will not be going there alone.
And she will be the helper.
So she said she has-- she has to discuss it with a friend of hers that she's with them, and she agreed.
So-- so I went with her, myself, and her friend, and his friend to that kolhoz.
So we arrived to that kolhoz, myself and three people with me, assuming that they were going to accompany us, two of us, to this village.
It took us three or four days there to-- we were waiting for the chairman of the kolhoz.
In the meantime, we don't live any place.
We just sit in this office, sit in a sitting position, sleep like the same-- the same kind of a way, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and wait.
So came [INAUDIBLE] that handled that kolhoz, [INAUDIBLE] is his name.
And he said he doesn't need a doctor because they have doctor, doctor's office was just opposite their kolhoz.
So I said to him, well, sign to me on a piece of paper that you-- that you don't honor that-- that-- that they send me here.
And I'll go back to-- to the-- to the office, and they will send me somewhere else.
So he was afraid to sign because they are terribly afraid of such things there in the Soviet Union.
We got the piece of paper, so-- so he agreed.
And so this way we remained in the kolhoz, and these two young men left to-- to town.
And we-- me and [?
Cooker ?] is her name.
Her real name was Rachel Alpert.-- Alpert, A-L-P-E-R-T.
That's '41.
It's in '41, in June '41, I went to Odessa.
In September-- in August, I went to Pereiaslav.
In September, I went to Syzran and from Syzran, to-- to Ufa in the-- in the fall.
In November, I went to Tashkent.
And this is where we are now, '41.
I-- I don't think that you're going to get what you want to get from this half-hour.
I will summarize to you the whole thing so that you will-- I did work in this kolhoz two years, from '41 to '43.
I learned the Uzbek language, because otherwise it would have been medicine like animals you treated, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I learned very fast their language.
It's a Turkic group of languages.
And pretty soon, they didn't know what to do with me, really.
Was ?] treated me very well after a while, after the bad beginning and so on.
And it was- they didn't want me to leave.
They wanted me to stay there forever, and so on.
And I had an interesting time, and I was only thinking about how will I to tell my-- my family what all I did and how I managed my life, and all that.
No.
No contact whatsoever.
No contact whatsoever.
[INAUDIBLE] just was waiting.
But how will I be able to stand it to meet them?
And so I was there two years.
Then they started to-- to require doctors to go to Ukraine.

So in that-- in that [NON-ENGLISH], in Tashkent again, I have here [INAUDIBLE] some of those documents, the head of the health-- of the personnel department put me in-- they required doctors to come to Moscow.
The requirement was to come to Moscow, and then they will send me to Ukraine.
But I told them I'm going to go to Moscow.
So I got the permission.
I got the permit, and I left two years later, Tashkent.
And I came to Moscow.
I came to Moscow.
Then there was a problem-- that they wanted us to go to-- to Ukraine.
In Moscow, you don't have a right to leave unless you have work.
To have work, you have to have place to live.
So there was-- was all that things how it went, and how I managed to be in Moscow.
And I was-- stayed in Moscow and worked in the hospital for-- [RUSSIAN].
It means expertness in being able to work.
People from all Moscow were sent to that hospital to decide whether they have-- can change their work or not.
That was an interesting-- as a neurologist, I worked.
And it was an interesting job, because you had to make a diagnosis about what's the matter with a person, and these were difficult cases.
Well, so I worked in this hospital.
In the meantime, I met Polish people from Warsaw.
And I met some-- one of them that I was the doctor in Warsaw, his son.
These were those rich communists from Warsaw.
Some of them got to-- to Moscow.
And it was called Zwiazek Patriotio Polskich.
They're the Zwiazek.
That's the Association of Polish Patriots.
So through these Polish Patriots, I-- I got the possibility to leave Moscow to Poland because I knew foreign languages.

They needed someone that would be able to interpret [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to interpret-- to-- the foreign correspondence what was going on because people started-- to do-- foreign correspondence started to come to Russia and then to-- to [? new ?] Poland.

So I left Moscow on the second plane that left Moscow as this translator, not as a doctor, because that's what the man that was in charge of the newspaper that was [? followed ?] in Poland needed a translator and [? decide ?] [? for there ?] I have acquaintances.
I had acquaintances that wanted to-- to help me get out of the Soviet Union because I thought they wouldn't let me out.
Everybody still asks, me how they did you leave?
There was a war of '44.
In '44, I came to Lublin.
In Lublin, I was exactly at the time when they did free Majdanek, that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Camp Majdanek.
I don't know whether you know.
Sure.
I interviewed them, those who had left Majdanek at that time.
Nine children were among them.
I interviewed those kids.
So that-- that I am already in Poland.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from Lublin, in a couple of weeks, I was sitting by myself in the radio, and I was the writing down how the Russian, what did they conquer, every single village, and so on, how they were making progress to-- on the front line.
Then, you know, was this Polish Warsaw Uprising thing.

And pretty soon, after a couple of weeks that I functioned as that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] translator, I went to Lodz, where I got my job as a senior assistant at the university clinic, and in neurology I was working.
In the meantime, from Paris came [INAUDIBLE], who was a husband of a friend of mine with whom I shared a-- a room in-- in-- in Warsaw.
She was married to him.
I knew them from Warsaw.
So they used to bring French people.
Yeah.
So I got the-- I got the job in-- in Poland in-- in [NON-ENGLISH] Lodz, a senior assistant at the clinic.
And I had a job as a medical director of the school, Public School of Labor Administrators.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So he, that [INAUDIBLE], was French, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- in the French embassy.
He was attache-- [INAUDIBLE] cultural attache.
So he was inviting Polish intellectuals to-- to Paris in exchange of-- some kind of an exchange they had.
And he offered me to go to-- to-- to Paris on such a fellowship for-- for three months.
So I left Poland in 1946-- July 1946, and I am in-- in Paris.
How did you experience the end of the war?
The war-- the war is still on.
Now, in '46, it was [INTERPOSING VOICES], but the war was still on when I came from-- well, I found out in Moscow that nobody is alive from my family.
I heard-- I heard about it from some people.
Such things happened, but nobody-- I wrote the letter to one of the doctors in Lutsk and received a letter back that among the living is none-- nobody from my family.
How-- how I experienced it-- I experienced it awful.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I remember very well the day when I learned about it.
So we are rushing.
We are rushing.
So you want me to put myself into that position of what I felt that-- that particular day?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I say I'm rushing with this.
I'm-- I'm rushing with what you want me to rush with.
[INAUDIBLE] you know?
I know it's important.
I mean, it was-- I thought I'm going to lose my mind.
That's what I thought.

I was sitting there by myself the whole day, and everybody-- everybody, a few people-- that knew me there and this cousin of mine there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went the next day to work, because I was working at this hospital.
But it meets like a part of me was cut off from me.
But as they say, I'm not-- I'm not in a-- in a position right now with the rush that we have to put myself into that.

And this is what I was thinking, that essentially, if you want to have this real in-depth interview to make sense [INAUDIBLE] the whole war, which has nothing to do maybe [INAUDIBLE] with-- it has nothing to do with the kind of thing that you are interested in, because for me, part of it, like going with them to Majdanek and translating to them, or visiting the Chopin Street store, where the Germans had put eyeglasses, which you knew that they were a pair of eyeglasses belong to a human being, or toothbrushes, or whatever.
And the German exact way as they usually did things, to go through this particular-- or to-- to have the interviews with the children that I mentioned that I have made--
But I'm telling it to you like it's a summary, not putting my-- my feelings and all that.
To do it in a rush, rush, rush way, that's not-- that's all what I can say.
It's not-- it's not that I'm not telling you what's-- what's happening or what I saw, but I'm not-- I'm not putting my emotions into it, not-- not as [INAUDIBLE] as I-- as I should, maybe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were a lot-- a lot of-- of such-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] such moments that we have to gloss over it.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
It's not that I don't feel, but-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] well, so what-- what else would you like to know?
I was asking, how did you notice that the war ended?
The war ended.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I think I was in Lodz when the war ended.
I was in Lodz at that time when the war ended.
And for me, it didn't end.
If you want to know, really [INAUDIBLE], I still think about it.
[INAUDIBLE] For me, it didn't end.
[PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]
So in accordance with what you wanted me to do, it-- we wanted to get to a firm in Paris.
Just one second.
